topic: Someone finds an unsigned birthday card in their mailbox
raw_output:
[story 1]

The mail slot rattled, a familiar, mundane sound that usually signaled junk flyers or bills. Today, however, something caught my eye. Tucked between a glossy advertisement for a new streaming service and a stack of utility statements was a thick, cream-colored card. It was completely blank on the front, just a crisp white square, with elegant, looping script printed on the inside: "Happy Birthday." My fingers, usually quick to dismiss anything unexpected, paused mid-reach. I picked it up, turning it over. No address, no signature. Just the sentiment. I folded it once, then unfolded it again, scrutinizing the paper for any hidden watermark or strange ink bleed. A slow, deliberate frown creased my brow. Who would send this? And why? I tucked the card into my wallet, a slight tension tightening my jaw as I walked toward the trash bin, intending to discard the oddity immediately.

[story 2]

I stared at the card as if it were a foreign object dropped onto my desk. It was an unsigned birthday card, resting innocently on my cluttered kitchen counter amidst dried pasta and forgotten grocery receipts. My initial reaction was a deep sense of unease. I picked it up with two fingers, turning it slowly under the harsh overhead light. The message inside was simple, almost too simple: "Wishing you the best." I tapped the paper lightly, the sound unnervingly loud in the quiet apartment. I looked around the room, checking the door, half expecting someone to materialize. My breath hitched slightly. I flipped the card over, examining the texture of the paper, searching for any telltale sign of forgery or a prank. It felt too deliberate, too out of place. I set it down abruptly, my gaze fixed on it, a knot forming in my stomach that refused to loosen.

[story 3]

The mailbox was overflowing, a daily testament to the sheer volume of the world's correspondence. Amidst the usual deluge, a single piece of mail stood out: a birthday card. It was pristine, utterly devoid of any sender's name or return address, yet it carried a distinct, almost formal handwriting. I picked it up, my movements stiff. I held it up to the light, trying to decipher any clue—a faint smudge, a watermark, anything to confirm its legitimacy. My lips pressed into a thin line as I read the message: "May your day be wonderful." I let out a short, humorless puff of air. It was absurd. I shook my head once, a small, almost imperceptible movement. I didn't put it in the recycling. Instead, I placed it carefully on the edge of my desk, watching it, waiting for some logical explanation to surface, though none seemed forthcoming.
